Hello there, fellow tea farmer from Hatton! I'm here to help you identify and manage Blister Blight, a common disease in our high-altitude tea gardens.

**Symptoms:**

* Severe blistering on leaves and stems
* Brown necrotic blisters that can spread quickly
* Leaves turn yellow, curl, distort, and drop
* Stem distortion, bending, and dieback
* Widespread shoot dieback

**Treatments:**

* Urgent treatment with systemic fungicides like tridemorph or contaf (apply every 5-7 days for 2-3 weeks)
* Use triazoles (e.g., hexaconazole) to avoid resistance
* Cultural treatment:
	+ Heavy pruning of infected shoots (remove 15-20 cm below symptoms)
	+ Regulate nitrogen fertilizers to prevent succulent growth

**Practical Tips:**

* Monitor your tea gardens regularly for early signs of Blister Blight.
* Consult with experts to check for resistance in your tea plants.
* Use bioagents consortia (Bacillus + Trichoderma) for long-term management.

**Safety Note:** Always wear protective gear, including gloves and masks, when handling fungicides. Follow the recommended dosages and application schedules to avoid harm to yourself, your workers, or the environment.

Hello from Hatton, Sri Lanka!

**Blister Blight Symptoms:**

• Small pinhole-sized spots on young leaves (less than a month old)
• Spots slightly enlarge but don't turn into blisters yet
• Mild on a few shoots
• Favored by cool and humid weather conditions

**Treatments:**

• Cultural treatment:
	+ Prune plants to improve air circulation
	+ Regulate shade to 40-60%
	+ Predictive spraying based on weather forecasts (if >80% RH for 3 days)
• Biocontrol treatment:
	+ Apply Trichoderma spp. or Bacillus-based bioagents as a preventive foliar spray (1-2 g/L water, twice monthly)

**Practical Tips for Local Farmers:**

* Monitor young shoots weekly to catch the disease early
* Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce humidity
* Improve air circulation and prune plants regularly

**Safety Note:** Always wear protective gear when handling chemicals or bioagents. Wash your hands thoroughly after use.

Hello from Hatton, Sri Lanka! I'm here to help you with the Brown Blight disease on your tea plants.

**Symptoms of Brown Blight:**

• Small oval pale yellow-green spots appear on leaves.
• A yellow halo forms around each spot.
• No concentric rings are visible (yet).
• Affected areas are minor, mostly on stressed or mature leaves.
• Weather favoring the spread: wet and warm conditions (20-28°C).

**Treatments for Brown Blight:**

• Cultural:
  • Give balanced NPK nutrients, avoiding excess nitrogen.
  • Improve drainage to reduce plant stress.
  • Monitor only if isolated outbreaks are found.

• Preventive:
  • Avoid overhead irrigation to prevent waterlogged areas.
  • Leave space between plants for good air circulation.

**Practical Tips for Local Farmers in Hatton:**

* Regularly inspect your tea plants, especially after heavy rainfall or when the weather is warm and wet.
* Maintain proper soil pH and nutrient levels according to soil test results.
* Implement integrated pest management (IPM) practices to minimize chemical use.

**Safety Note:** Always wear protective gear when handling chemicals. Make sure you follow local regulations and safety guidelines for pesticide application.
